narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 48% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your academic profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 25, Year 1 school decile ranking of 3, and Year 3 decile of 2—all of which are below the typical student's averages of around 3.21 for GPA, 36.7 for LSAT, 5.71 for Year 1 decile, and 5.53 for Year 3 decile. Your middle family income bracket, female gender, Asian racial background, and plan to attend full-time are also factored in, though these align more closely with common distributions in our dataset, where full-time students make up over 90%, women about 44%, and Asian students around 6%. When the model evaluated your application, your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure because it's notably lower than average. Right behind that was your LSAT score, which similarly nudged the odds toward failure due to its significant gap from the norm. Your Year 1 decile came next in influence, again contributing in the direction of predicted struggle, followed by family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status, each playing a progressively smaller role but still tilting slightly against success. Overall, these academic performance indicators—especially your later-year grades and test scores—were the key drivers, outweighing your demographic factors. Compared to typical admitted students, who tend to have stronger high school and undergrad metrics, your profile showed patterns linked to bar passage challenges in our data. The good news is that several of these aren't set in stone for your bar prep journey. To boost your future chances, focus on intensive bar exam preparation, perhaps through targeted tutoring to address the study habits reflected in your lower deciles, and consider practicing LSAT-style logic intensively since test performance was a big factor. Building a stronger academic track record now, like excelling in any remaining law school courses, could also help demonstrate growth. With dedicated effort, many students overcome similar predictions—keep pushing, and you can rewrite this story.
